text: The liquefaction of sand and soil may affect the lateral stiffness of the axle system, thereby influencing the safety of train operation on the bridge under seismic action. Therefore, it is crucial to understand the impact of sand liquefaction on the dynamic response of the axle system in order to accurately assess the operation safety of trains on the bridge in the liquefaction site. This paper first develops a Midas to OpenSees program based on the Python language (referred to as MTO), and establishes a rapid establishment method for the full-bridge dynamic model of OpenSees. Taking a certain (88+168+88)m prestressed concrete continuous rigid frame bridge as an example, by using the MTO program, the Winkler foundation beam is adopted to simulate the group pile foundation, and the pile-soil interaction and the reduction coefficient ψ of the mechanical index of liquefied soil are considered to simulate the liquefaction of sandy soil. By comparing and analyzing the changes in the dynamic characteristics of Bridges under different liquefaction conditions, a foundation has been laid for the subsequent safety research of high-speed railway axle systems.

In the simulation of sand and soil liquefaction, the 'm method' was adopted to consider the pile-soil interaction, and the reduction coefficient ψ of the mechanical index of liquefied soil was introduced by referring to the 'Code for Seismic Design of Railway Engineering' (GB 50111-2006,2009 Edition) to simulate the liquefaction process. The full-bridge finite element models of different group pile foundations were established by using Midas Civil, and the OpenSees and TTBSAS models were generated through the MTO transformation program for comparative analysis. The results show that the bridge vibration modes under non-liquefaction conditions are the same as those under different liquefaction depths and degrees, with the main features being transverse bending and longitudinal drift. After considering the liquefaction of sand and soil, the natural vibration period of the structure is prolonged, and the increase in liquefaction depth and degree will lead to a significant extension of the lateral period of the bridge, indicating that liquefaction has a significant impact on the dynamic characteristics of the bridge.

In the case of earthquake-induced sand liquefaction, the Midas Civil finite element model was imported into the TTBSAS calculation program in the study to analyze the influence of different liquefaction depths and liquefaction degrees on the dynamic response of the train-rail-bridge coupling system. The results show that with the increase of liquefaction depth and degree, the lateral dynamic responses of Bridges, tracks and vehicles all increase. Taking a vehicle speed of 250km/h as an example, compared with the 10m condition at a liquefaction depth of 20m, the mid-span lateral displacement of the bridge, the lateral displacement of the rail and the lateral acceleration of the vehicle's center of mass increase by 13.38%, 21.96% and 9.46% respectively. The degree of liquefaction ψ=1.00 compared with no liquefaction ψ=0.00, with increases of 37.02%, 99.31% and 15.49% respectively. Meanwhile, the analysis shows that the influence of sand liquefaction on the axle system is mainly reflected in the transverse direction, while the vertical influence is relatively small.

The liquefaction of sand and soil induced by earthquakes has a significant impact on the operation safety of continuous rigid frame Bridges of high-speed railways. Study and analyze the influence laws of liquefaction depth and liquefaction degree on derailment evaluation indicators and safe vehicle speed thresholds. The results show that an increase in the depth and degree of liquefaction will lead to an increase in the evaluation indicators, but as the train speed increases, the influence of liquefaction gradually weakens. Under vibration-free conditions, all the safety indicators of train operation are less than the limit values, indicating that the requirements are met. Under seismic conditions, not considering liquefaction will overestimate the safe speed threshold. For example, under the indicator 1 derailment coefficient, the safe speed thresholds with or without liquefaction are 250km/h and 275km/h respectively. The safety speed thresholds for the lateral horizontal force of the indicator 3 wheel pairs are 200km/h and 225km/h respectively. Research shows that liquefaction has a significant impact on the safety of train operation on Bridges and must be fully considered in design and evaluation.

In this paper, the Winkler foundation beam is adopted to simulate the group pile foundation, and the pile-soil interaction is considered through the 'm method'. Meanwhile, referring to the 'Code for Seismic Design of Railway Engineering' (GB 50111-2006, 2009 Edition), the reduction coefficient Ψ of the mechanical index of liquefied soil is introduced to simulate the liquefaction of sandy soil. Based on the existing program TTBSAS system, the influence of earthquake-induced sand liquefaction on the dynamic response of high-speed railway axle systems was studied, and the following conclusions were obtained: Firstly, with the increase of liquefaction depth, the lateral displacement at the mid-span of the bridge in the axle system significantly increases, and the variation law of lateral acceleration is also related to the train speed. Under the calculation conditions of this paper, when the vehicle speed is 250 km/h, the lateral displacement of the bridge in the middle span under the conditions of liquefaction depth of 10 m and 20 m increases by 20.1% and 38.6% respectively compared with the condition without liquefaction. Secondly, as the liquefaction depth increases, the driving safety indicators at different vehicle speeds all increase, but the increase is less than the lateral displacement in the mid-span of the bridge. Meanwhile, as the vehicle speed increases, the influence of liquefaction depth on the safety of train operation on the bridge gradually weakens. Thirdly, the influence law of the degree of liquefaction on the dynamic response of the axle system is basically consistent with that of the depth of liquefaction. As the degree and depth of liquefaction increase, the structural stiffness decreases, the cycle extends, and the displacement increases, thereby reducing the safety of train operation on the bridge. Fourth, for the train derailment coefficient index and the wheel set lateral horizontal force index, not considering the liquefaction of the site sand and soil would overestimate the train's safety when crossing the bridge. Among them, when considering and not considering the liquefaction of sand and soil, the safe speed thresholds of the derailment coefficient index are 275 km/h and 300 km/h respectively. The safe speed thresholds for the lateral horizontal force index of the wheelset are 200 km/h and 225 km/h respectively. This paper preliminarily studies the influence of earthquake-induced sand and soil liquefaction on the dynamic response of high-speed railway axle systems, and suggests that the role of sand and soil liquefaction be considered in the design of high-speed railway bridges in liquefaction sites. Due to the complexity of the axle system, simplified methods were adopted in both the simulation of pile-soil interaction and sandy soil liquefaction in this paper. More accurate simulation methods still require further research.

Why is sand liquefaction closely related to earthquakes? On the earth's surface, saturated and loose sandy soil is a complex composed of sand grains and water. Sand grains maintain their stability and withstand pressure through the friction between grains, while water cannot be compressed in volume and can withstand extremely high pressure and flow, but it cannot withstand shear force. Before an earthquake, the external forces on the sandy soil layer are mainly borne by the sand skeleton, and the sand layer remains stable. During a strong earthquake, the shear force generated by seismic vibration causes sand grains to slide and change their arrangement. The stress is transferred from the sand framework to the pore water, resulting in an increase in the pressure of the excess pore water. Eventually, liquefaction occurs, manifested as sand ejector and water seepage, and sand ejector channels are formed on the surface.

The phenomenon of sand and water gushing during an earthquake is the result of liquefaction of the underground sand and soil layer. When an earthquake occurs, the sand and soil below the groundwater level are instantly subjected to a huge seismic force. The interstitial water in the sand and soil layer cannot be discharged in time, and the pressure of the interstitial water suddenly rises, causing a significant change in the properties of the sand and soil layer, showing characteristics similar to liquids. This phenomenon is called sand and soil liquefaction disaster. Sandblasting and water gushing are the most obvious macroscopic signs of sand liquefaction. Under the pressure of the upper soil layer, liquefied sand will emerge from the ground from areas with weak coverage. In severe cases, large areas of farmland and crops will be buried, and channels and Wells will be silted up. Bank embankment collapses often occur in areas where the cover layer of river channels, highways and railway ditches is weak. After the sand layer liquefies, due to the existence of open surfaces, it often leads to the subsidence, cracking and collapse of riverbeds, embankment embankments and roadbeds, and may also cause serious damage to Bridges and other facilities. Ground cracking and subsidence are also significant manifestations of liquefied sand and soil. Liquefied sand and soil may eject from ground fissures, simultaneously intensifying ground cracking. The re-deposition of the liquefied sand layer further aggravates the damage to the superstructure. The occurrence of sand liquefaction is closely related to the type and state of the soil. Historical data shows that in areas with severe sand and water gushing, the groundwater level is generally shallow, usually not exceeding 3 meters. When the groundwater level is between 3 and 4 meters, the phenomenon of sandblasting and water gushing rarely occurs. When it exceeds 5 meters, almost no cases are seen. Therefore, the maximum depth of the groundwater level for liquefaction can be set at 5 meters. The characteristic index boundaries of modern liquefied soil are as follows: average particle size 0.02 to 1 mm, coefficient of non-uniformity less than 10, relative density less than 75%, and plasticity index less than 10. By sorting out and analyzing the data of sandblasting and water gusping during earthquakes in China over nearly 900 years before 1955 and the relationship between the epicenter distance and magnitude, it is concluded that as the magnitude of the earthquake increases, the liquefaction range expands. For example, the liquefaction range of a magnitude 7 earthquake can reach 100 km, and that of an magnitude 8 earthquake can reach 200 km. When the magnitude is less than 5, no liquefaction phenomenon is found. Therefore, sand liquefaction mainly occurs in strong earthquakes. The liquefaction disaster of sand and soil directly affects the urban construction and development in our country and is an important link in seismic safety assessment, seismic fortification and earthquake damage prediction. Judging from disastrous earthquakes such as the Tangshan earthquake, the Osaka earthquake, the Hualien earthquake in Taiwan, the earthquake in Turkey and the Wenchuan earthquake, the liquefaction of sand and soil has caused extensive and serious losses to mankind.

China's complex geographical location, land area and geological topography make it one of the countries with the most frequent continental earthquakes in the world. In the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake, almost all the main affected areas witnessed varying degrees of liquefaction, resulting in the collapse of a large number of houses and Bridges. Research shows that the liquefaction of site sand and soil and weak foundations are among the important reasons for the failure of Bridges under seismic action. From 2010 to 2040, the state plans to connect major provinces and cities across the country by railway, forming a large framework of the national railway network. The national railway mileage has reached 146,300 kilometers. Bridges account for a high proportion in high-speed railways. For example, Bridges account for 94.2% in the Guangzhou-Zhuhai Intercity Railway. The seismic resistance capacity of Bridges directly affects the safety of high-speed railways under seismic action. Geological surveys show that China's high-speed railway Bridges pass through large areas of liquefiable soil. These areas are very likely to undergo liquefaction during strong earthquakes, seriously affecting the safety of Bridges and the normal operation of railways. Therefore, it is very necessary to conduct in-depth research on the interaction of liquefiable site systems under strong earthquakes. When dynamic loads act on saturated cohesive soil in a liquefaction site, the superstatic pore water pressure within the soil increases. Once it reaches the normal stress value on the shear surface, the soil loses its shear strength and presents a liquid-like state, which is known as sand liquefaction. The seismic design code for railway Bridges suggests the use of group pile foundations in liquefaction sites. Group pile foundations can effectively prevent the overall instability of the bridge, but the liquefaction of sandy soil will still reduce the lateral constraints of the soil on the group piles, thereby changing the lateral stiffness of the bridge. As the axle system is sensitive to the lateral stiffness of the bridge, the liquefaction of sand and soil induced by earthquakes may further affect the safety of train operation on the bridge. The essence of the problem of sand and soil liquefaction is the change of bridge boundary conditions, which belongs to the problem of pile-soil interaction. Research shows that the interaction between piles and soil can affect the amplitude of the dynamic response of bridge structures, and this influence may threaten the safety of high-speed railway Bridges and the trains on them. Therefore, to accurately assess the operational safety of trains on Bridges and on liquefaction sites, it is necessary to have a thorough understanding of the impact of sand liquefaction on the dynamic response of the axle system, which is crucial for ensuring the safe operation of high-speed railways.

Saturated fine sand and silt sand are complexes composed of sand and water. Figure 1 shows a schematic diagram of the liquefaction process of saturated sandy soil: (1) Before an earthquake occurs, the external force is mainly borne by the sand framework, and water only bears hydrostatic pressure. At this time, the sandy soil foundation is stable, as shown in Figure 1(a); (2) After being repeatedly subjected to ground motion loads, sand particles move and their arrangement changes, while the volume of saturated sand remains unchanged. The ground motion loads are transferred from the original sand framework to the water body, causing a sharp increase in pore water pressure. When the pore water pressure is equal to or greater than the total stress borne by the soil, saturated sand liquefies, the soil structure is completely destroyed, sand particles are suspended in the water and eject from the weak parts of the ground fissures, forming the phenomenon of water and sand seepage, as shown in Figure 1(b). (3) After liquefaction, the suspended sand grains gradually sink, the sand framework is damaged, the bearing capacity of the foundation is lost, and surface settlement and tilting occur, as shown in Figure 1(c).

The conditions for the formation of liquefaction in sandy soil foundations include both external and internal factors. The main external cause is the effect of earthquakes. The internal factors mainly include the following aspects: First, the density of the sand and soil is low. Actual investigations show that the particle size and gradation of sand and soil are important factors affecting liquefaction. Sand and soil with uniform particles and smooth surfaces are more prone to liquefaction. The density of sand and soil is the fundamental factor affecting dynamic stability. When the relative density (Dr) is less than 70%, liquefaction often occurs, while when Dr Is greater than 70%, liquefaction generally does not occur. Second, the viscosity between particles is relatively low. Particles with a diameter less than 0.005mm are called clay particles. Due to their interaction with water, they have special properties and possess original cohesion (viscosity). The sandy soil framework with a low content of clay particles is relatively fragile due to the lack of viscosity and is prone to liquefaction. Therefore, silty clay (10<IP≤17) foundations generally do not liquefy easily, while silty soil (3≤IP≤10) foundations may liquefy. Thirdly, the sand and soil are in a saturated state and lack drainage conditions. Only in a saturated state can sand and silt undergo liquefaction, and loose sand and silt can reach a saturated state below the groundwater level. Therefore, groundwater and its water level are important conditions affecting liquefaction.

The forms of subgrade damage caused by liquefaction mainly include settlement and longitudinal cracks, transverse cracks, as well as collapse and flow. Firstly, the main causes of subsidence and longitudinal cracks, apart from the insufficient density of the embankment itself, also include poor bearing capacity of the foundation. And the liquefaction of sand and soil is one of the important reasons for the poor bearing capacity of the foundation. For instance, during the Tangshan earthquake, the embankment at the No. 82 bridge of Shiliu River on the Jingshan Line was approximately 4 meters high, with the maximum subsidence reaching 3.4 meters. The subsidence lengths were 350 meters and 100 meters respectively. The main cause of the subsidence was the liquefaction of the fine sand deposited in the river channel, which was as deep as 6.3 meters. The spillage on both sides of the embankment is extremely severe. The trees at the foot of the slope tilt towards the embankment, and the embankment body cracks longitudinally and sinks into the soil. Secondly, transverse cracks often occur at places buried underground with pipe culverts and other foreign objects, but their number is relatively small and they are not the main damage caused by earthquakes. Finally, the collapse and flow are mainly manifested as the partial sliding out of the embankment, piling up at the foot of the slope or flowing out to a more distant area. The former mostly occurs in areas with intense vibration, while the latter mostly happens in liquefied soil regions. For instance, during the 1964 Nankai Earthquake in Japan, the embankment of the Hanetsu Line was 8 meters high and about 100 meters long, filled with sand dunes, and the lower part of the embankment was flooded. During the earthquake, sand and soil flowed, with the farthest outflow reaching 10 meters, and the volume of collapsed soil reached 15,000 cubic meters.

For soil layers that may be liquefied, the excavation and replacement method should be adopted as much as possible. When excavation is difficult or uneconomical, artificial densification measures can be considered first to make the soil reach a compact state that is compatible with the designed seismic intensity, and then combined with other protective facilities. Specific measures include: First, the dynamic compaction method. Through the free fall of a heavy hammer, a huge impact energy is applied to the soil in an extremely short period of time. This energy is converted into compression waves, shear waves, and Rayleigh waves, etc., forcing the soil to be compressed, vibrated dense, drained and consolidated, and pre-compressed and deformed, thereby making the soil particles tends to a stable state, eliminating liquefaction and reinforcing the foundation. Second, the vibration tube compaction method: After the tube is drilled, the packing is compacted into piles by vibration or hammering, and then the packing is forced into the soil by mechanical high-frequency forced vibration. Thirdly, the pile foundation should be used to penetrate the liquefiable soil layer, allowing the pile tip to extend into the stable underlying layer. The extension length should not be less than 0.5m for gravel soil, gravel, coarse and medium sand, and hard clay soil, and no less than 1.0m for other non-rocky soils. Fourth, reduce the load, enhance the overall rigidity and uniform symmetry of the superstructure, set settlement joints reasonably, and avoid using structural forms that are sensitive to uneven settlement.

The domestic standard method based on in-situ testing is an empirical approach derived from previous seismic experience statistics. Although it lacks a theoretical basis, it is simple and practical, and easy for engineers to master. In the initial judgment, important factors such as the basic intensity of the field earthquake, soil layer age, clay particle content, and groundwater were comprehensively considered. Ultimately, it was determined that the liquefaction and its degree were quantitatively calculated and classified based on the test results such as the fortification intensity, clay particle content, benchmark strike number or static exploration resistance, and corresponding treatment measures were taken. The NCEER method, Robertson and Olsen's method compare the equivalent periodic stress ratio (CSR) with the periodic resistance ratio (CRR) of the foundation soil to determine the liquefaction of sandy soil. They fully consider various influencing factors such as site conditions, clay content, and magnitude. After correcting the gauge penetration number and static exploration resistance, the seismic stress comparison is conducted. It is a widely adopted method abroad at present. The domestic standard method for judgment is more conservative than that abroad. In foreign-related projects, fully understanding the design intent, different discrimination methods can be adopted to meet different needs, accurately predict and determine the liquefaction of sand and soil, and take corresponding treatment measures to strengthen the concept of overseas projects. Methods such as the NCEER method or the Robertson method have not been explicitly stipulated in domestic norms and regulations. More engineering practices are needed to test and summarize them. Their reasonable application can help Hong Kong Engineering survey and design personnel become familiar with and master the international standard system for determining sand liquefaction, and further enhance the core competitiveness of survey and design business.

Domestic ports usually adopt the standard penetration test discrimination method mainly based on JTS 146-2012 'Code for Seismic Design of Water Transport Engineering' to determine the liquefaction of site sand and soil. The normative method is based on the initial determination of the sedimentary age and the content of clay particles. It compares the measured value of the standard penetration N63.5 with the critical value of the penetration hammering number Ncr calculated for liquefaction discrimination. If N63.5 is less than Ncr, it is judged that liquefaction will occur; otherwise, it will not. Within a depth range of 20 m underground, the critical value of the number of hammer strikes for liquefaction discrimination criteria can be calculated by the formula Ncr = Nα β [ln(0.6ds + 1.5) - 0.1dw] 3Mc, where Ncr is the critical value of the number of hammer strikes for liquefaction discrimination criteria penetration, and Nα is the reference value of the number of hammer strikes for liquefaction discrimination criteria penetration. β is the adjustment coefficient. For the design of earthquakes, the first group is set at 0.8, the second group at 0.95, and the third group at 1.05. ds is the standard penetration depth of saturated soil (m), dw is the depth of the groundwater level below the ground surface (m), and dw is set at 0 when the ground is underwater. Mc is the clay content. When it is less than 3%, it is sand and soil, and 3% is taken. This normative method has become the main principle for identifying the liquefaction of sand and soil in domestic water transport projects. However, the method lacks a theoretical basis, and the identification results for deep foundation soil are rather conservative.

The NCEER method is a simplified experimental analysis method based on the Seed method. Its judgment is based on the fact that when the shear stress generated by seismic vibration is greater than the shear stress required to trigger liquefaction, sand liquefaction may occur. Seed simplifies this theory and compares the equivalent periodic stress ratio CSR with the periodic resistance ratio CRR of the foundation soil to determine the liquefaction of sandy soil. That is, when CRR > CSR, the sandy soil does not liquefy; otherwise, it does.

The periodic stress ratio (CSR) is calculated based on the basic seismic design parameters of the site. Currently, the calculation expression proposed by Seed et al. is widely accepted. Considering the influence of earthquake magnitude, CSR is converted to the equivalent CSR7.5 at magnitude Ms = 7.5 through the magnitude ratio coefficient, that is, CSR = 0.65·(σ0 / σ'0)·amax·rd, where CSR is the seismic cyclic stress ratio and 0.65 is the correction factor. σ0 represents the total stress of the soil at the calculated depth, σ'0 represents the effective stress at the calculated depth, amax represents the peak acceleration of the ground, and rd represents the stress reduction coefficient. The calculation formula for the stress reduction coefficient rd is as follows: when the depth h of the calculation point is ≤ 9.15m, rd = 1.0-0.00765·h; When 9.15 < h ≤ 23 m, rd = 1.174-0.0267·h.

The internal cause of sand liquefaction mainly lies in the physical properties of the sand itself, while the external cause is the seismic intensity. Through theoretical analysis and on-site statistical data on seismic sand liquefaction, it can be known that the larger the values of the three factors, namely the standard penetration impact number N63.5, the average particle size d50, and the overburden effective pressure σv, the stronger the sand's ability to resist seismic liquefaction, and this restrictive relationship is basically linear. Therefore, these three factors can be used as important indicators for evaluating the anti-liquefaction capacity of sandy soil.

Existence of air in the pores of soil is considered to enhance liquefaction resistance. Air in the pores plays a role in absorbing generated excess pore pressures by reducing its volume. Change in volume of the pore fluid, that is, the air-water mixture, may be the factor dominating this mechanism. Considering a soil mass with its pores filled with air and water, for a small change in the pore pressure Δp, we obtain the volumetric strain of the soil using Boyle's law as εv = Δp / (p0 + Δp) (1 − Sr) e / (1 + e) ≤ σc' / (p0 + σc') (1 + Sr) e / (1 + e) ≡ εv*. Here, p0 and e denote the pressure of the fluid and the void ratio of the soil mass, respectively. In the above equation, compressibility of soil grains and water is ignored. The highest value of the volumetric strain for a soil is achieved when Δp attains its possible maximum value, which is equal to the effective confining stress σc'. This highest value of volumetric strain is hereafter termed the potential volumetric strain, εv*. Effects of the factors appearing in this equation were individually investigated through a series of triaxial tests on Toyoura sand specimens at a relative density of 40%. Three testing parameters, including the initial effective confining pressure σc', the back pressure, and the degree of saturation Sr, were varied between tests while the void ratio of the specimens was kept constant throughout the test series. It should be noted that the back pressure p0 is the absolute pressure instead of the ordinarily used gauge pressure. It was found that the liquefaction resistances of the partially saturated sand increase with the initial confining pressures, with the liquefaction resistances being higher for lower Sr. Also, the liquefaction resistance depends on the back pressure; the liquefaction resistance decreases as the pressure increases (Okamura, Soga, 2006). The liquefaction resistance ratio, which is the liquefaction resistance of partially saturated sand normalized with respect to that of fully saturated sand, is plotted against the potential volumetric strain. All the data lie along a unique curve, confirming that the potential volumetric strain is the determining factor of the effect of the degree of saturation on this specific sand at a relative density of 40%. It was also found that data retrieved from the literature, obtained from tests on specimens prepared using different sand at different relative densities and different confining pressures, lies along the same curve (Okamura, Soga, 2006). This indicates that the effect of the degree of saturation on liquefaction resistance is dominated by the factor εv*.

When the thickness of the liquefied layer remains unchanged, with the increase of the liquefaction degree, the time-history waveforms of the lateral displacement and lateral acceleration in the mid-span of the bridge are similar. Especially when the reduction coefficients Ψ of the mechanical indicators of the liquefied soil are 0.33 and 0.66, they are almost exactly the same as those without liquefaction (Ψ is 0.00). When the reduction coefficient Ψ of the mechanical index of liquefied soil is 1.00, the time-history waveforms of the lateral displacement and lateral acceleration at the mid-span of the bridge change, and the peak moments are also different. Meanwhile, with the increase of liquefaction degree, the peak values of lateral displacement and lateral acceleration in the span of the bridge tend to increase.

(1) With the increase of liquefaction degree, the amplitude of lateral displacement in the mid-span of the bridge all increases, but the increase rate is not uniform. Among them, when the vehicle speed is 250 km/h, the reduction coefficients Ψ of the mechanical indicators of liquefied soil are 0.33, 0.66 and 1.00 respectively. The increase in the lateral displacement of the bridge in the middle span under the working conditions of Ψ 0 is 1.7%, 7.2% and 38.7% respectively, and the increase gradually increases. (2) Under different degrees of liquefaction, when a train passes over a bridge at different speeds, the variation law of the amplitude of the lateral acceleration in the middle of the bridge span is rather complex. Among them, when the reduction coefficient Ψ of the mechanical index of liquefied soil is 0.33 and 0.66, the amplitude of the lateral acceleration at the mid-span of the bridge is larger than that without liquefaction, and the higher the degree of liquefaction, the greater the amplitude of the acceleration. When the reduction coefficient Ψ of the mechanical index of liquefied soil is 1.00 and the vehicle speed increases from 200 km/h to 300 km/h, the amplitude of the lateral acceleration at the mid-span of the bridge is first less than that in the non-liquefied condition and then gradually increases. (3) By comparing with the aforementioned Figure 6, it can be found that the variation law of the amplitude of the lateral acceleration at the mid-span of the bridge with the vehicle speed under different liquefaction degrees is similar to that of the amplitude of the lateral acceleration at the mid-span of the bridge under different liquefaction depths. The larger the reduction coefficient Ψ of mechanical indicators of liquefied soil, the higher the degree of liquefaction of sandy soil, the weaker the constraint effect of the soil around the piles on the bridge group pile foundation, and the longer the natural vibration period of the structure. Therefore, the influence of liquefaction degree on the dynamic response of Bridges is similar to that of liquefaction depth. Essentially, it reduces the stiffness of the structure and prolongs the structural period, which will significantly increase the displacement response of the bridge. In addition, the reasons for the above-mentioned changes in the amplitude of the bridge's lateral acceleration are also consistent with the influence of the liquefaction depth. Further investigate the impact of the degree of liquefaction on the safety of train operation on the bridge. The amplitude changes of driving safety indicators 1 to 5 under different liquefaction degrees are shown in Figure 11.
